Gulp Series: Gulp Is Not a Drink — It's a Task Runner (Intro)

Gulp was the first frontend static-site tool I used when I started learning frontend. People often mix up Gulp and Webpack — I did too. Before I wrote my Webpack post, I thought Webpack was basically Gulp, but writing both made it obvious they're really different things. So let me introduce Gulp here. I genuinely like developing with it.
The Gulp logo is a cup of drink:

You'll run into Gulp in a lot of places. So what is it? Gulp is a task automation tool that runs tasks in sequence. Think of it like finishing one cup of drink before you can have the next. Some people describe it as a plumber, because Gulp pipes outputs from one task into the next:
Gulp vs Webpack is a classic comparison. If you're curious, check out
Frontend Build Tools — Webpack vs Gulp (article in Mandarin)
For a frontend dev, learning another tool means more options. The next post starts the actual install and writing. Before that, prep:
A text editor (I recommend VS Code)
Gulp runs on Node.js, so install that first.
(Use LTS, not Current — Current can hit weird issues.)

LTS: Long-Term Support, stable
Current: Latest, less stable
